title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Claddock discusses the medical history of the late Mr. Crickenshaw with Dr. Campbell. Claddock inquires if the doctor suspects poisoning, noting that Crickenshaw had previously claimed the doctor suspected someone was poisoning him. Dr. Campbell admits that while he officially diagnosed Crickenshaw with severe gastroenteritis, the symptoms were consistent with arsenic poisoning. However, the doctor observes that these symptoms only manifested when the entire family was present for dinner, whereas they did not occur when only the household staff were present. Dr. Campbell also mentions that a former partner, Mr. Morris, suggested these suspicions might be unfounded because the symptoms did not align with the administration of regular, small doses of arsenic. As Claddock considers whether someone might benefit from the death of Luther Creech, Dr. Campbell defends Emma, noting the pattern of symptoms occurring only during large gatherings.

The conversation is interrupted by Alexander and Stoddard, two children who claim to have found a significant lead. To avoid being disturbed, the boys lead Claddock to a cluttered tack room near the stables. They explain that after searching bushes, hollow trees, and garbage cans, they discovered a dusty, crumpled envelope inside a zinc tank in the boiler room, which Mr. Hillman had been using to burn scrap paper. Stoddard presents the envelope to Claddock, which is addressed to Madeline Crickenshaw at an address in France. Alexander suggests this proves Edmund's French wife has visited the estate, while Stoddard speculates that she might actually be the person in the coffin.
